coredata.dc:title: One-pot green synthesis of BSA-capped O-CQDs as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of promethazine drug
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Viji, S.
Dinesh, A.
Radhakrishnan, K.
Priya, L. Srimathi
Sivasankari, C.
Santhamoorthy, Madhappan
Ayyar, Manikandan
Mohanavel, V.
Hashem, Mohamed
Fouad, Hassan
Ramachandran, G.
Santhoshkumar, S.
coredata.dc:description: Reliability in biosensing technologies is crucial for the accurate detection and measurement of target molecules, prompting strategies to minimise non-specific binding events that may result in inaccurate findings. This work reports on bovine serum albumin (BSA)-capped oxygen rich carbon quantum dots (O-CQDs) to improve the accuracy of promethazine sensing. The CQDs were produced utilizing a hydrothermal technique with apple juice as a sustainable carbon source. BSA-Caped O-CQDs improve their biocompatibility and stability in physiological conditions while also reducing non-specific interactions with other biomolecules. Several spectroscopic methods, such as fluorescence, X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), were used to evaluate this enhanced specificity and sensitivity. Obtained results show that BSA-O-CQDs had higher specificity for promethazine Furthermore, their ability to resolve non-specific binding was assessed under simulated physiological backgrounds, demonstrating their promise for robust biosensing applications. This sensor method achieved a high selectivity with LOD of 0.2 μM from range of 10–90 μM indicating its suitability for precise promethazine measurement in environmental samples. The primary advantages of this technique include simplified sample preparation together with economic advantages which make it a critical instrument for public health monitoring as well as cutting-edge pharmaceutical research. Sustainable O-CQDs demonstrate the potential for creating environmentally friendly biosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
BSA-O-CQDs
Fluorescent sensing
Promethazine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent analysis for detection of promethazine
Selectivity and Sensitivity analysis for Promethazine (PMZ) Detection
Research data policy and data availability statements

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM analysis of BSA-O-CQDs, (a) TEM images showing spherical in nature with an average size range of 4–8 nm; (b) HRTEM images displaying distinct lattice fringes, (c) SAED patterns revealing concentric rings and (d) Histogram of particle size distribution with an average particle size of about 7 nm.
XRD patterns of BSA-capped oxygen-rich carbon quantum dots (BSA-O-CQDs).
FTIR analysis of BSA-capped oxygen-rich carbon quantum dots (BSA-O-CQDs).
XPS analysis of BSA-O-CQDs): (a) Comprehensive XPS spectra showing peaks, (b) C1s spectrum, (c) O1s spectrum, and (d) N1s spectrum analysis, respectively.

Optimization and stability analysis of BSA-O-CQDs, (a) Fluorescence intensity as a function of pH in PBS solutions, (b) Stability of fluorescence intensity in varying NaCl concentrations, (c) Temperature stability demonstrating consistent fluorescence intensity; (d) Long-term stability over 60 days, with retention of fluorescence intensity, respectively; (e) incupation time for Rapid response to promethazine (PMZ) detection (f) Photostability under continuous UV irradiation.

Displays the results of selectivity and sensitivity analysis of BSA-O-CQDs towards promethazine (PMZ): (a) The selectivity of BSA-O-CQDs towards PMZ, as compared to other interfering compounds and metal ions, is shown by a significant change in fluorescence intensity exclusively for PMZ. (b) The concentration-dependent quenching behaviour of BSA-O-CQDs is demonstrated by the fluorescence quenching as PMZ concentrations increase from 0 to 200 μM. (c) A non-linear Stern-Volmer plot for PMZ quenching is shown. (d) A linear Stern-Volmer plot for PMZ quenching in the concentration range of 0 to 90 μM is shown, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9905 and an estimated limit of detection (LOD) of 0.2 μM.
Diagram representing the hydrothermal synthesis process of BSA-O-CQDs for sensing PMZ
Comparative analysis for PMZ fluorescent sensors.
Detection of Promethazine (PMZ) from environmental samples.

head.ce:title: One-pot green synthesis of BSA-capped O-CQDs as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of promethazine drug

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reliability in biosensing technologies is crucial for the accurate detection and measurement of target molecules, prompting strategies to minimise non-specific binding events that may result in inaccurate findings. This work reports on bovine serum albumin (BSA)-capped oxygen rich carbon quantum dots (O-CQDs) to improve the accuracy of promethazine sensing. The CQDs were produced utilizing a hydrothermal technique with apple juice as a sustainable carbon source. BSA-Caped O-CQDs improve their biocompatibility and stability in physiological conditions while also reducing non-specific interactions with other biomolecules. Several spectroscopic methods, such as fluorescence, X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), were used to evaluate this enhanced specificity and sensitivity. Obtained results show that BSA-O-CQDs had higher specificity for promethazine Furthermore, their ability to resolve non-specific binding was assessed under simulated physiological backgrounds, demonstrating their promise for robust biosensing applications. This sensor method achieved a high selectivity with LOD of 0.2 μM from range of 10–90 μM indicating its suitability for precise promethazine measurement in environmental samples. The primary advantages of this technique include simplified sample preparation together with economic advantages which make it a critical instrument for public health monitoring as well as cutting-edge pharmaceutical research. Sustainable O-CQDs demonstrate the potential for creating environmentally friendly biosensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: First-generation antihistamine, Promethazine (PMZ) serves for various therapeutic functions and shows a widespread clinical usage. It possesses multiple useful pharmacological characteristics including anti-nausea and anti-vomiting effects and mechanisms for blocking histamine receptors and treating psychosis components while delivering pain relief and sedation effects [1–3]. In addition to hemorrhoids, pneumonia, allergic reactions, motion sickness, and mental health issues, patients require medical treatments for neoplastic illnesses and respiratory infections [4,5]. Promethazine is a drug that treats aging processes, inhibits sperm activity, and prevents the synthesis of horizon melanin. Promethazine's potential as a COVID-19 treatment has been investigated by medical and pharmacy researchers. PMZ has serious adverse effects, including heart issues, endocrine abnormalities, immunoallergic reactions, venous thrombosis, central nervous system depression, severe respiratory problems, and sudden infant death syndrome, even if it is a helpful drug [6–8]. The FDA regulatory notifications bring attention to issues such as tissue damage caused by intravenous usage and life-threatening respiratory depression in young infants [9]. The imperative need for stringent regulation and supervision is demonstrated by the significant increase in promethazine misuse for non-medical intentions. Due to the extensive utilization and possible misuse of promethazine (PMZ), it is essential to implement strict monitoring measures.

The conventional methods for analysis of (PMZ), includes gas chromatography (GC), liquid chromatography (LC-MS/MS), spectrophotometry, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), colorimetry and turbidimetry includes chemicals, solvents, complicated machinery, and extensive sample processing [10–15]. Electrochemical methods including voltammetry and potentiometry present a useful solution that combines cost-effectiveness with high selectivity. The performance of voltametric procedures require glassy carbon electrodes to undergo either screen printing modification or replacement with electrodes that include functional elements and nanoparticles. The ion-selective electrode represents a highly effective class of potentiometric sensors [16]. Spectrophotometry demonstrates useful solution evaluation capabilities yet proves inadequate as a field detection method. The analysis of complex mixtures benefits from conventional separation methods even though they present various operational limitations [17]. Laborious work together with detailed sample pre-processing needs long analysis durations along with premium equipment and specialized analysts and expensive instruments.

The field of nanotechnology produced new nanomaterials that possess unconventional characteristics. Discovery of carbon dots (CDs) in 2004 raised significant interest because of their remarkable benefits including low price along with selective fluorescent outcomes, harmless nature, compatibility with biological systems, strong water dissolution and competitive affordability relative to both quantum dots and organic dyes. The unique characteristics of CDs formed an ideal basis for their implementation across photocatalysis sensing polymerization cell-labeling techniques and studying ionic activity [18–21].

Researchers used microwave-assisted synthesis, laser ablation, hydrothermal synthesis and ultrasonic methods to synthesis Carbon Dots. Different synthetic methods require varying starting materials consisting of citric acid combined with kappa carrageenan and glycine and melamine [22,23]. The sustainable synthesis of CDs becomes feasible through cost-effective along with eco-friendly biomass resources. Scientists have researched three different starting materials including mushrooms, gram shells and turtle shells for making carbon dots [24–26].

Biomass serves as an essential element in carbon dot (CD) synthesis while supporting sustainable processes and leveraging unused materials per green chemistry standards [27]. Researchers investigate agriculture waste utilization through ultrasonication techniques for CD synthesis as a cutting-edge method to convert biowaste into functional nanomaterials. Research studies demonstrate the synthesis of carbon dots using biomass materials consisting of banana peels alongside coffee grounds and orange peel thus demonstrating how biomass-derived CDs boost environmental sustainability and processing versatility [28–30]. The application of jute caddies marked a distinct research approach since their use demonstrates how industrial waste can create valuable nanomaterials [31]. The synthesis approach preserves the environment and results in CDs with specific features enabling applications in sensing while expanding drug delivery functions and antibacterial properties and photocatalytic and light emitting diode capabilities to facilitate sustainable nanotechnology development.

The sensing systems utilize CDs to display a strong fluorescence properties. Certain functional probes detect various substances at high precision levels to identify changes in pH and biological molecules while maintaining selectivity constraints. The unique combination of functionalization properties and biocompatible characteristics enables CDs to function as components in medication delivery methods. Through controlled release technology scientists modify material surfaces to introduce multiple medications for specific drug delivery uses. CDs activate antibacterial properties by creating reactive oxygen species after photoexcitation [32]. Antibacterial materials are outstanding for therapeutic applications due to their demonstrated antibacterial functions. Threaded applications within photocatalysis enable light-based reactions to degrade pollutants. The activity of effective photocatalysts with adjustable electronic properties enables efficient electron transfer. Carbon dots provide superior emission capabilities than all named LEDs. Carbon dots act as affordable environmentally friendly replacements for traditional xylene processing methods [33–35]. The production of carbon dots from biomass enables sustainable manufacturing approaches alongside multiple opportunities for use in scientific machinery and ecologically oriented applications and biomedical approaches [36,37].

The objective of this study is to investigate a new approach for detecting promethazine by utilizing oxygen-rich carbon dots (O-CQDs) capped with bovine serum albumin (BSA). BSA-O-CQDs were first produced by utilizing apple juice as a sustainable carbon source in a hydrothermal procedure. The use of BSA enhanced the specificity and sensitivity of the sensor towards promethazine. The use of BSA is crucial for enhancing sensor performance due to its unique affinity for promethazine molecules, which minimizes interference from other ions. The technique of selective binding enhances the accuracy of the sensor, even in the presence of potential interferences. This enhances its dependability for use in a broad spectrum of therapeutic and environmental applications. The approach has several practical advantages, such as simpler sample preparation and increased sensitivity, enabling the reliable and accurate identification of promethazine.

Resources utilised in the sample analysis were sourced from various chemical suppliers. The apple utilised in this study was sourced from the local market in Coimbatore, India. SRL India supplied citric acid with a purity of 99.5 % and quinine sulphate dihydrate. Central Drug House (P) Ltd. provided pharmaceutical ingredients from India for our research, including promethazine (PMZ), adenosine triphosphate (ATP), phenergan (PHG), ribavirin (RBV), acyclovir (ACV), ibuprofen (IBU), lidocaine (LID), and theophylline (THP). Magnesium (Mg2+), manganese (Mn2+), potassium (K+), sodium (Na+), and aluminium (Al3+) were sourced from Sigma-Aldrich Pvt. Ltd. in India. The company acquired four compounds from Merck Life Science Private Limited in India: Procaine (PRC), Isoniazid (INH), and Paracetamol (PCM). The substances used were not subjected to additional purification as they were acquired commercially. A solution utilised for the analysis was prepared using water sourced from the Milli-Q water purification system by Millipore.

One-pot hydrothermal processing allowed the synthesis of typical apple juice derived BSA-capped O-CQDs. The capping function of Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) helps stabilize and boost the light emitting properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Initially, we cleanse all the glassware with aqua regia and hydrochloric acid in a ratio of 3:1 prior to using ethanol and ultrapure water. Apple juice was subjected to filtration processes to produce pure solution without solid contaminants. Followed by 20 mg of citric acid and 15 mL of apple juice solution were combined with 20 mL of ultrapure water. Bovine serum albumin solution formation require 100 mg BSA dissolution in 20 mL of ultrapure water and added to above mixture to develop the CQD precursor solution was stirred for 30 min. The final precursor solution was packed in Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for a duration of 12 h. The obtained BSA-wrapped CQDs was yellow-brown color during their chemical formation process. The obtained BSA-O-CQDs was storage under 4 °C conditions for 24 h.

Heavy particles created sedimentation that accumulated at the bottom of the storage container are observed at low temperature. The identified impurities together with unreacted precursors were removed through supernatant separation. The fluorescence-stable purified BSA-O-CQDs product maintained its proper form. The unintentional mixing separation occurring during cold storage time worked as a natural purification step. The dispersion observed at room temperature storage to avoid sedimentation while ensuring long-term stability.

Characterization techniques were used to examine the synthesized bovine serum albumin (BSA)-capped oxygen-rich carbon quantum dots (O-CQDs) along with their promethazine sensing application. We analysed high-resolution image carbon dots using a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope (Japan) at 200 kV accelerating voltage. The BRUKER D8 Advance DaVinci X-Ray Diffractometer with Cu Kα irradiation (λ = 1.54060 Å) was used to determine the crystalline structure through XRD investigations. An X-ray diffraction device operates with 2θ scan parameters between 10° and 80° at a scan speed of 0.05° per second. The Thermo Fisher DRS-FTIR instrument measured surface functional groups extending across the 4000–400 cm−1 frequency range. Surface analysis and elemental data regarding the material were obtained through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). A SPECS PHOIBOS 150 MCD with Al Kα X-rays activated this process. We used a JASCO V-770 spectrophotometer to examine the optical properties through a UV–Vis Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (DRS) measurement. The PL spectra evaluation for BSA-O-CQDs emission response occurred using the FLUOROMAX Spectrofluorometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent analysis for detection of promethazine
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of promethazine occurred through analysis with BSA-O-CQDs in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at standard room temperature and pH at 8. Preparation of the PBS solution with 10 mM concentration at pH 8 started the experimental process. Reagents were used to achieve pH 8 of the sensing medium before final dilution to 1 L. A 0.22 m filter was used for solution filtration. The sensing system was prepared by adding 10 mL BSA-O-CQDs in 1 L PBS solution to achieve fine dispersion of BSA-O-CQDs with effective fluorescence intensity. The Promethazine solutions consisted of stock solutions, which required deionized water dilution to reach 4 mL total volume in a concentrations from 0 to 200 μM. Promethazine was incubated at room temperature for 10 min to analysis the emission intensity of BSA-O-CQD. The fluorescence intensity was excited at 380 nm and recording emission spectra within the range of 380–600 nm using a 5 nm slit width settings. Similarly experiment was assessed for various interfering molecules to quantify changes in fluorescence intensity at specific concentrations.

The detection accuracy for promethazine improved through a complete evaluation performed using BSA-O-CQD. The prepared real sample was filtered through 0.22 μm filter system and 10 mM phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at pH 8. The accurate sensing of promethazine in tap, pond and river water sourced under natural environment. Table 2 reports the exact amounts of promethazine spiked for analysis. The unique sample preparation methods along with specialized surface-cleaning protocols to target residue removal for production processes. The precise measurement and identification of promethazine required running samples through a 5 % w/v NaCl solution held at 40 °C.

For the purpose of determining the quantum yield (QY) of BSA-O-CQDs, we make use of the traditional reference method that comprises quinine sulfate. In order to calculate the quantum yield, it is necessary to compare the total fluorescence intensities and absorbance of the BSA-O-CQDs with those of the quinine sulfate reference. BSA-O-CQDs excited at a wavelength of 380 nm, the quantum yield (QY) of quinine sulfate in a solution of 0.1 M H2SO4 is 18 %. For the purpose of calculating the quantum yield (QY) of BSA-O-CQDs, the following equation:
(1)
Q x
=
Q std
I x
/
I std
h x
2
/
h std
2
A std
/
A x
where the quantum yields of quinine sulfate (Qstd) and N-GCDs (Qx) are two different substances. With the symbols hx 2 and hstd 2, respectively, we may represent the water and H2SO4 refractive indices. Ix and Istd are the abbreviations for integrated emission zones. The symbols Ax and Astd represent the optical density of quinine sulfate dye and N-GCDs, respectively. According to the findings, the quantum yield of N-GCDs is 18 %.

The synthesis method includes the preparation and characterization of BSA-capped oxygen‑carbon quantum dots (BSA-O-CQDs). The BSA-O-CQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method. The interaction of citric acid and apple juice led to the formation of a polymeric substance via condensation polymerization. After the carbonisation process, the polymer-like substance transformed, leading to the effective use of BSA-O-CQDs for the sensing of PMZ, as illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The shape and size distribution of BSA-O-CQDs were analysed through TEM analysis. The average size range was 4–8 nm, with TEM images predominantly displaying well-stabilized spherical nanoparticles capped by BSA, as illustrated in Fig. 1(a). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images exhibited clear lattice fringes, with an inter-planar spacing of about 0.21 nm, confirming the crystalline structure of the carbon core in the quantum dots, as illustrated in Fig. 1 (b). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns exhibited concentric rings, indicating a polycrystalline structure of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1(c)). The average particle size of BSA-O-CQDs was approximately 7 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 1 (d).

Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of BSA-O-CQDs that were analysed. A prominent peak in the XRD pattern was observed at 2θ = 24.1°, which is the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. This suggests that graphitic domains are present in the CQDs. In addition, a peak that corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon was seen at 2θ = 42.4°. These peaks suggest that the CQDs have some degree of crystallinity despite their small size and oxygen-rich surface functional groups. A crystalline structure is indicated by the well-defined graphitic planes, which is essential for the possible applications of CQDs in bioimaging, sensing, and catalysis, where these structural features can improve performance.

The successful surface modification was demonstrated by the formation of many distinctive peaks corresponding to different functional groups in the FTIR spectroscopic analysis of BSA-O-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 3 . The presence of hydroxyl and amine groups, which are characteristic of BSA, is shown by the strong peak seen at 3316 cm−1. The stretching vibrations of the OH and NH bonds are represented by this peak. The existence of carbon‑hydrogen bonds within the carbon quantum dots and BSA is suggested by the signal at 2990 cm−1. The stretching vibrations of aliphatic and aromatic groups are linked to this peak. The identification of BSA is evidenced by the peak at 1639 cm−1, associated with the CO stretching vibrations of amide. The signal at a wavenumber of 1448 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibrations of CH bonds, indicating the presence of aliphatic chains. The peak observed at a wavenumber of 1408 cm−1 is attributed to the flexing oscillations of OH bonds, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups. The signal at 1089 cm−1 indicates CO stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of alcohol or ether groups associated with the oxygen-rich functional groups on the CQDs. The presence of amine groups in the BSA coating is confirmed by the peak at 1048 cm−1 corresponding to CN stretching vibrations. The peaks observed at 885 cm−1 and 641 cm−1 indicate CH out-of-plane bending vibrations, a characteristic feature commonly associated with aromatic compounds. The FTIR investigation confirms the incorporation of various functional groups via BSA capping, thereby enhancing the biocompatibility, stability, and functionality of the CQDs. This is important as these features can be utilised in sensing, bioimaging, and catalysis.

The analysis of BSA-O-CQDs surface composition and elemental features emerged from an X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) examination. The XPS spectra data (Fig. 4a) exhibited three major peaks at 284 eV for C1s along with 399 eV for N1s and 531 eV for O1s. XPS analysis showed that BSA-O-CQDs contained nitrogen (N) and carbon (C) as well as oxygen (O) elements in their surface composition.

The extended XPS spectrum from C1s (Fig. 4b) revealed peak intensities at 284.6 eV, 286.5 eV and 288.7 eV. A spectroscopic analysis distinguished peaks associated with graphitic or aliphatic CC/CC, CN, and CO functional group bonds. Two distinct oxygen peaks emerged at 531.8 eV and 532.7 eV in O1s spectroscopy results (Fig. 4c). The detected peaks connect to oxygen-containing functions CO and COH/COC. The N1s spectroscopy (Fig. 4d) produced three detectable peaks at electron volt (eV) values of 399.4, 400.6, and 401.4 eV. The spectral peaks arranged in CNC then N(C) followed by NH occurred in the spectrum.

The research confirmed that BSA-O-CQDs contain oxygen doping within their chemical composition as carbon-dots. Hydroxyl, amide, amino and carboxyl/carbonyl functional groups make BSA-O-CQD nanocrystals dispersed in water so they have a wide range of applications for sensing in aqueous solutions.

To identify the optimal pH for the sensor system, we analysed different phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solutions across a pH range of 2 to 12. The results presented in Fig. 5a indicate a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity as pH rises from 2 to 8. At pH 8.0, the fluorescence intensity attained its peak and remains constant in more alkaline conditions. The presence of promethazine (PMZ) resulted in the most significant quenching of fluorescence intensity within the pH range of 7 to 8. This optimisation illustrates the importance of adjusting the pH environment to attain optimal sensor performance, as the medium's pH significantly influences both quenching efficiency and fluorescence intensity.

Fig. 5b illustrates that the fluorescence intensity of BSA-O-CQDs remains largely stable despite an increase in NaCl concentration to 0.20 M. This demonstrates that BSA-O-CQDs exhibit significant stability under conditions of elevated salt concentrations. Thus, they are expected to demonstrate efficacy in biological samples or environments with diverse ions. Their limited response to variations in ionic strength renders them appropriate for a diverse array of biological and ecological sensing applications, especially in contexts where salt presence is considerable and sensor performance must be preserved.

Fig. 5c indicates that the fluorescence intensity of BSA-O-CQDs is stable across temperatures from 10 to 70 degrees Celsius. The findings indicate that the fluorescence intensity of BSA-O-CQDs remains remarkably stable despite significant temperature fluctuations. The findings indicate that maintaining the BSA-O-CQDs at room temperature enhances their performance. BSA-O-CQDs exhibit remarkable thermal stability, enabling consistent high performance across diverse environments.

A comprehensive investigation was conducted to evaluate the stability of BSA-O-CQDs over a 60-day period, in order to determine their viability for long-term applications. The data indicated that BSA-O-CQDs exhibit significant stability over a 60-day period, maintaining their fluorescence intensity. The experimental approach entailed the storage of BSA-O-CQDs in a controlled environment at room temperature, with regular measurements of fluorescence intensity conducted using a fluorescence spectrometer. After 30 and 60 days, there was approximately 98 % and 95 % retention of fluorescence, respectively, indicating no significant decrease in intensity. The results indicate the potential of BSA-O-CQDs for long-term applications, particularly in contexts necessitating sustained sensor performance, as illustrated in Fig. 5d. The slight decline in fluorescence intensity and sensing capabilities of BSA-O-CQDs demonstrates their reliability and durability, rendering them suitable for various environmental and clinical monitoring applications. The 60-day stability study demonstrates their suitability for prolonged use, thereby enhancing their practicality and reliability as fluorescence sensors. An assessment was conducted to evaluate the optimisation time for the practical application of BSA-O-CQDs in the detection of promethazine (PMZ). The introduction of 100 μM PMZ into the sensing system facilitated this achievement. Fig. 5e illustrates that PMZ rapidly decreased the fluorescence intensity by 50 % within a 30-s interval. The highest degree of quenching efficiency was recorded within a maximum duration of 60 s. The results highlight the swift and efficient response of BSA-O-CQDs to PMZ, demonstrating their potential for use in rapid and dependable sensing applications.

The photostability of BSA-O-CQDs was conducted under continuous UV irradiation to ensure their reliability in applications. Fluorescence intensity measurements were conducted regularly to assess stability during the exposure of BSA-O-CQDs to UV light at a constant intensity. Fig. 5f illustrates the results indicating that BSA-O-CQDs (Bovine Serum Albumin-capped Carbon Quantum Dots) exhibit significant stability. Over a 90-min period, the fluorescence intensity exhibited minimal fluctuation, remaining largely stable. The stable fluorescence signal underscores the structural stability of BSA-O-CQDs and validates their applicability in real-world scenarios involving prolonged UV exposure. Their longevity is evidenced by the minimal variation in fluorescence intensity, rendering them suitable for various applications in bioimaging, photodetectors, and environmental monitoring. This study illustrates the remarkable photostability of BSA-O-CQDs, ensuring their effectiveness and structural integrity under challenging conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and Sensitivity analysis for Promethazine (PMZ) Detection

ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of BSA-CQDs towards promethazine (PMZ) over other ions, we examined the photoluminescence (PL) intensity changes in the presence of various interfering compounds. The concentration of each interfering compound was standardized to match the conditions under which the selectivity was being tested. The interfering compounds included adenosine triphosphate (ATP), phenergan (PHG), ribavirin (RBV), acyclovir (ACV), ibuprofen (IBU), lidocaine (LID), theophylline (THP), procaine (PRC), isoniazid (INH), paracetamol (PCM), and urea (UR), each at a concentration of 60 μmol L−1, while PMZ was present at a concentration of 90.0 μmol L−1. Additionally, the metal ions aluminium (Al3+), lead (Pb2+), sodium (Na+), potassium (K+), magnesium (Mg2+), nickel (Ni2+), copper (Cu2+), and manganese (Mn2+) were tested. The results demonstrated that only promethazine (PMZ) caused a substantial change in the fluorescence intensity of BSA-CQDs, indicating a high selectivity for PMZ detection. In contrast, the presence of other interfering compounds resulted in negligible changes in PL intensity, suggesting minimal non-specific interactions in Fig. 5(a). The high selectivity of BSA-CQDs towards PMZ can be attributed to the unique interaction between the carbon quantum dots and promethazine. This interaction likely involves specific binding sites on the surface of BSA-CQDs that preferentially interact with the promethazine molecules, leading to a distinct quenching or enhancement of the fluorescence signal. The negligible interference from other compounds indicates that BSA-CQDs have a strong affinity for PMZ, making them a reliable probe for detecting promethazine in complex samples.

To assess the sensitivity of BSA-O-CQDs towards promethazine (PMZ), we investigated varying concentrations of PMZ from 0 to 200 μM. The fluorescence quenching of BSA-O-CQDs clear demonstrated a concentration-dependent quenching behaviour for PMZ. As illustrated in Fig. 6b, there is a gradual decrease in photoluminescence (PL) intensity at λex = 380 nm with increasing PMZ concentrations. The intrinsic spectral peak of the BSA-O-CQDs-PMZ complex remains unchanged relative to that of the BSA-O-CQDs. The fluorescence of the BSA-O-CQDs is almost entirely quenched at a PMZ concentration of 200 μM, indicating a high sensitivity of the BSA-O-CQD-based sensing system to PMZ concentration.

The fluorescence quenching data were examined by applying the Stern-Volmer equation to determine whether dynamic or static quenching processes were at play. The eq. F/F0 = Ksv[Q] + 1 represents the relationship between the PL intensities of BSA-O-CQDs in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of PMZ, where Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of PMZ. Fig. 6c demonstrates that the Stern-Volmer plot does not exhibit a linear correlation over the whole range of concentrations. Fig. 6d demonstrates a strong linear association (correlation value R2 = 0.9905) within the concentration range of 0 to 90 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) for PMZ was determined by employing the given equation: Utilizing the LOD = 3σs plot approach, where s is the calibration plot's slope and σ is the recorded blank BSA-O-CQDs sample's relative standard deviation (n = 3). Significantly, the limit of detection is 0.2 μM. This finding suggests that BSA-O-CQDs are capable of efficiently identifying PMZ in deionized water. Table 1 summarises a comparison of LOD and linear range with several reported CD probes for PMZ detection and demonstrates that our LOD is lower than the majority of stated values.

The sensing mechanism of the BSA-O-CQDs sensor for detecting promethazine involves a complex interplay of molecular interactions and photophysical processes. Bovine serum albumin-functionalized oxidized carbon quantum dots (BSA-O-CQDs) serve as the sensing platform, where the presence of promethazine induces a significant quenching of photoluminescence (PL). This quenching phenomenon is primarily attributed to the formation of a complex between promethazine and BSA-O-CQDs, facilitated by specific binding interactions.

Upon excitation at 380 nm, BSA-O-CQDs emit a distinct PL signal, which is quenched as promethazine molecules interact with the BSA-O-CQDs surface. The mechanism involves a photo-induced electron transfer process, where electrons are transferred from the excited state of BSA-O-CQDs to promethazine molecules, thereby reducing the PL intensity. Experimental observations, including a linear response to promethazine concentrations ranging from 10 to 90 μM and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.2 μM, corroborate the sensor's high sensitivity. Furthermore, the negligible variation in the sensor's response in the presence of ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA), a metal ion chelator, confirms the specificity of the BSA-O-CQDs sensor for promethazine detection. This mechanism not only underscores the robustness of BSA-O-CQDs as a versatile sensing platform but also highlights its potential for abroader application in environmental and biomedical analyses through further functionalization for selective analyte detection.

This study assessed the efficiency of BSA-O-CQDs for the detection of promethazine (PMZ) ions through fluorescence quenching in various water samples, including tap, pond, and river water. The experimental techniques adhered to established protocols for sample preparation and sampling to maintain consistency with distilled water assessments. Various concentrations of PMZ were introduced to the BSA-O-CQDs, and the resulting quenching of fluorescence intensity is documented in Table 2 . The persistent quenching effect observed in all analysed water samples indicates the sensor's sensitivity and adaptability to diverse aquatic conditions. Recovery rates varied between 98 % and 99.7 %, demonstrating high accuracy, while the study revealed minimal impact from other substances present in the water. The analysis demonstrates that the BSA-O-CQDs sensor effectively detects PMZ ions in real water samples with high accuracy and consistency. This characteristic renders it an essential instrument for the monitoring and evaluation of environmental applications.

This conclusion presents a highly sensitive fluorescence detection method for the selective identification of promethazine in water samples, utilizing BSA-O-CQDs exhibit a distinct fluorescence emission peak at a wavelength of 442 nm upon excitation with light at 380 nm. The sensor demonstrates high sensitivity and selectivity, as evidenced by the significant decrease in peak fluorescence intensity in the presence of PMZ. The measurement results indicated a detection threshold of 0.2 μM and a response range of 10 to 90 μM, demonstrating high reliability (R2 = 0.9905). The sensor exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity for PMZ molecules due to the numerous functional interfaces provided by BSA-O-CQDs, which enable the formation of strong hydrogen bonds with PMZ, facilitate π–π stacking, and permit electrostatic interactions. The sensor exhibited no performance decline when immersed in solutions with competing analytes or interfering ions in complex environmental matrices. The method illustrates the potential of BSA-O-CQDs in detecting promethazine in various environmental samples, as well as their ability to facilitate specific binding group modifications for an enhanced analytical scope. This results in the development of adaptable fluorescent sensors that are cost-effective and demonstrate consistent performance in environmental monitoring, pharmaceutical analysis, and advanced sensing systems.
Not applicable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research data policy and data availability statements
ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.

S. Viji: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. A. Dinesh: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. K. Radhakrishnan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. L. Srimathi Priya: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision. C. Sivasankari: Visualization, Validation. Madhappan Santhamoorthy: Visualization, Validation. Manikandan Ayyar: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. V. Mohanavel: Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Mohamed Hashem: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. Hassan Fouad: Visualization, Validation. G. Ramachandran: Visualization, Validation. S. Santhoshkumar: Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is funded by Researcher supporting project number (RSP2025R117), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
